text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: The 28×28 image (that little grid on the left with a "7"

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: drawn in amber pixels) gets flattened into a single column of

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: 784 neurons. Row by row, pixel by pixel, all spatial information

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: Then every single one of those 784 neurons connects to

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: every neuron in Hidden Layer 1 (128 neurons). That's already

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: 100,352 weights just in the first connection. Then 128×64 =

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: 8,192 more weights. Then 64×10 = 640 weights to the

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: The output layer has 10 neurons — one per digit. Softmax

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: converts the raw values into probabilities. The "7" neuron

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: Now — this works for 28×28. But think about what happens

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: when the image is 224×224 (standard real-world size).

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: That's 50,176 input neurons. First layer alone? 50,176 × 128

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: = 6.4 million weights. And it still has zero understanding of

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: We started with a 28×28 image. We applied 32 filters (each

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: 3×3). Each filter slid across the entire image and produced a

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: 26×26 feature map. So now we have 32 feature maps of

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: Each feature map is like a new "image" — but instead of

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: showing brightness, it shows "how strongly does my pattern

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: match at each location." One map highlights horizontal edges,

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: another highlights vertical edges, another highlights corners,

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: Think about it — the 26×26 grid has 676 values per feature

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: map. Across 32 maps, that's 21,632 values. And this is just

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: For a 224×224 image? Each feature map would be 222×222.

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: With 32 filters, that's over 1.5 million values. Still a lot.

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: "Do I really need all 676 positions? If I detected a

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: horizontal edge at pixel (10,10) and also at pixel (10,11) —

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: those are basically the same detection. The edge didn't move

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: The nearby values in a feature map are often very similar —

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: redundant information. You could shrink the grid without

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: How would you shrink it? You have a 26×26 grid of numbers

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: and you want to make it smaller while keeping the important

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: information. What's the simplest approach you can think of?

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: For example, if you have four neighboring values like:

text.font-family: Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji
Excalifont, Xiaolai, sans-serif, Segoe UI Emoji

g.text: Image Data koi bhi work nahi kiya hai?
